column2: Accelerated learning through AI‑Powered content transformation
column3: CRE Control used Copilot to transform a 90+ page newcomer orientation slide deck into an engaging onboarding video. This can significantly reduce the time required for new team members to understand key processes and boost the consistency and quality of knowledge transfer. Copilot enabled us to deliver a modern, efficient learning experience with minimal manual effort.
column4:
Step 1 - Upload relevant materials e.g. slide deck to Copilot - Create using this prompt: “Create a video from this document. Also use the notes and remarks next to each slide for the voice over narratives.”
Step 2 - Review and revise the drafted transcript, and make sure the sensitive information is adjusted
Step 3 - Adjust the voice of the narrative and music. Remove optional slides like chapter title page.
Step 4 - Export and share the video with the team
column6:
Risk: team structure or process change makes the video become outdated and newcomers receive obsolete information. 
Mitigation: video can be easily regenerated using Copilot.

column2: EXCEL- Excel Analysis within BI&C and FBP
column3:
This use case demonstrates how Business Insights & Control (BI&C) and Financial Business Partners (FBP) can leverage Microsoft 365 Copilot in Excel to enhance their financial analysis workflows. The focus is on automating data analysis, improving transparency, and accelerating reporting cycles using Copilot’s capabilities in data creation, editing, visualization, consolidation, and Python‑powered forecasting.
It showcases how Copilot supports the finance teams in KPI analysis, cost consolidation, and forecasting, enabling analysts and business partners to shift time from manual work toward value‑adding insights and decision support.
column4:
Task 1 – Analyzing Data

1.1 Get KPI insights

Using the following prompt:

  • “Summarize KPI trends and show data insights in bullet points. Name the countries with the highest sales.”

1.2 Analyze KPIs with pivot tables

Using the following prompt:

  • “Create a pivot table summarizing ‘Profit’ by ‘Month’ per ‘Country’. Also include ‘Gross Margin’ and ‘Customer Satisfaction’ as additional metrics. + Add to new sheet.”

1.3 Visualize KPI data

Using the following prompt:

  • “Visualize the ‘Sum of Profit’ per ‘Country’ in a pie chart. + Add to new sheet.”

Task 2 – Pulling & Consolidating Data

2.1 Generate formula & summarize Operating Expenses

Using the following prompt:

  • “Summarize the total Operating Expenses for each Country using the data in the ‘Cost Analysis’ sheet. Group the results by Country and show the output as a summary table with country names and their corresponding total Operating Expenses.”

2.2 Identify top cost categories (Top 3 countries)

Using the following prompt:

  • “Identify the top 3 Countries with the highest total Operating Expenses from the ‘Cost Analysis’ sheet. Use the ‘Operating Expenses’ column, aggregate the spend by country, and present the results in a descending order table showing country and total Operating Expenses.”

2.3 Filter & group data

Using the following prompt:

  • “Filter the data in the ‘Cost Analysis’ sheet to show only records where the Country is ‘Netherlands’ and the Profit is negative. Group the filtered results by Month and display a table with Month, Profit, and Operating Expenses.”

Task 3 – Enhancing & Reporting Data

3.1 Forecasting with Python (Sales & Cost Forecast)

Using the following prompt:

  • “Analyze the historical quarterly sales and cost data from the last 5 years in this table. Use Python to forecast sales and costs for the next 4 quarters. Explain the assumptions behind the forecast, highlight any anomalies in past data, and provide actionable recommendations to achieve a 10% increase in sales next year. Present the results as: A dual‑axis line chart comparing actual vs forecasted values.”

3.2 Executive summary creation

Using the following prompt:

  • *“Create an executive summary of the sales and cost forecast based on the historical quarterly data from the last 5 years. Include:
    1. Key trends observed in historical data
    2. Forecasted values for the next 4 quarters
    3. Main assumptions behind the forecast
    4. Risks and opportunities impacting accuracy
    5. Actionable recommendations to achieve a 10% sales growth target.
      Present the summary in a concise, professional tone suitable for senior management.”*

column2: EXCEL - computing formula with Copilot
column3:
Main goal: simplify manual calculations on excel.

Specifically:

Goal1: perform SUMIFS(YTD, Sector_View, "<>Allocations")/1000
Goal2: compute the difference between the obtained value and another value 

NOTE: You can adapt the prompts to perform any Excel formula. Simply ask Copilot to explain the formula you want in plain English, then select your data and apply the prompt.
column4:
Step 1 — Select the data

In Excel, select the table or data range on which you want to perform the SUMIFS calculation.


Step 2 — Use Copilot to calculate the SUMIFS

Use the following prompt: “Add up all YTD values for rows where Sector_View is anything except ‘Allocations’, then divide the total by 1000. Give me the result in the chat.”


This returns the YTD total (excluding Allocations), converted into thousands.


Step 3 — Select the data for the difference calculation

In Excel, select the table or range where you want to calculate the difference using the result generated in Step 2.


Step 4 — Use Copilot to calculate the difference

Use this prompt: “Make the difference between this result and the first value of the row ‘Total primary costs’ of tab Sheet2.”


This calculates: (SUMIFS result) – (first value of ‘Total primary costs’ in Sheet2)


NOTE: You can follow only the steps you need.

If you only require the SUMIFS/other complex Excel formula calculation, use steps 1 and 2. If you only need the subtraction/other immediate Excel formula between 2 values, use steps 3 and 4.


NOTE: You can adapt the prompts to perform any Excel formula. Simply ask Copilot to explain the formula you want in plain English, then select your data and apply the prompt.

column2: EXCEL - Add New Columns to tables
column3:
This use case shows how to use Copilot in Excel to add new columns to a table and automatically perform calculations.
The example focuses on variance calculations (Actuals vs MOR and Actuals vs TAP+), but the same method can be used for any type of calculation—differences, ratios, percentage changes, KPIs, quality checks, and more.
column4:
Step 1: Select the table you are working on in Excel.

Step 2: Use Copilot with this prompt:

'Add 2 columns: the first one  named 'Commited vs MOR' and the second one named 'Commited vs Tap+'.  'Commited vs MOR' column calculates Commited - MOR.  'Commited vs Tap+' calculates Commited - TAP+

Copilot will insert the new columns and automatically generate the correct formulas for each row.

column2: EXCEL - Apply Conditional Formatting to Simplify Data Analysis
column3:
This use case shows how Copilot can be used to analyze a table and automatically apply conditional formatting, helping users quickly identify patterns, issues, or items that require attention.
The example highlights valaues based on whether they are positive or negative. The same approach can be used for any type of data‑driven rule, such as thresholds, anomalies, outliers, or alerts.
column4:
Step 1: Select the table or column you want to analyze in Excel.

Step 2: Use this prompt:

“Analyze the values in the selected column and highlight them in yellow if positive and in red if negative.”

Copilot will automatically assess the data and apply the conditional formatting rules accordingly.

column2: EXCEL - Aggregate and Summarize Data  in Excel
column3:
This use case demonstrates how to use Copilot in Excel to quickly aggregate and summarize data, reducing manual grouping, pivoting, and formula work.

While the example focuses on summarizing Actuals by Country, the same approach can be applied to any metric (e.g., Forecast, Variance, Operating Expenses, KPIs) and any grouping field (e.g., Sector, BL, Region, Cost Center).
column4:
Step 1: Select the dataset you want Copilot to analyze (e.g., a table or a range in the ‘xxxx’ sheet).


Step 2: Use this prompt in Copilot:

“Summarize the total Actuals for each Country using the selected data in the ‘xxxx’ sheet. Group the results by Country and show the output as a summary table with country names and their corresponding total Actuals.”

Copilot will analyze the selected data, group values by Country, calculate totals, and generate a clean summary table.

column2: EXCEL - Data Quality & Readiness Check Before Analysis
column3:
This use case demonstrates how to use Copilot in Excel to automatically check the quality and readiness of a dataset before performing any analysis.
Copilot scans the selected table for issues that could break formulas, cause incorrect calculations, or distort reporting.
column4:
Step 1: Select the table you want to validate in Excel.

Step 2: Use this Copilot prompt:

“Check the selected table for data quality issues that would break analysis.
Return a table with: Issue type, Column, Example rows, Suggested fix.
Specifically check for: blanks in key fields, duplicates, inconsistent currency/date formats, text‑numbers, trailing spaces, and outliers.
Then propose a cleaned ‘recommended schema’ (headers + data types).”

Copilot will analyze the dataset, flag potential issues, present them in a structured summary table, and then propose a recommended cleaned schema.

column2: EXCEL - Build a Table and Apply a Consistent Design on Excel
column3: This use case explains how to use Copilot in Excel to convert a selected range into a structured Excel Table and apply a clean, consistent design.
column4:
Step 1: Select the range of data you want to convert into a table.

Step 2: Use this prompt in Copilot:

“Convert the selected range into an Excel Table. Use the first row as headers. Apply a green table style (green header row, banded rows if available). AutoFit columns so all headers are fully visible.”

Copilot will automatically create the table, assign headers, apply the chosen style, and adjust column widths.

column2: EXCEL - Change Cell Formatting
column3:
This use case explains how Copilot in Excel can quickly apply specific formatting to selected cells, helping users standardize their spreadsheets and improve data readability.
The example shows how to format numbers as Euro currency or apply a yellow fill color, but the same method can be used for any formatting action, such as borders, fonts, number formats, alignment, and cell styles.
column4:
Step 1: Select the cells you want to format in Excel.
Step 2: Use one of the following Copilot prompts:

Option A — Format as Euro currency
“Format the currently selected cells as Euro currency (€). Use 2 decimal places and a thousands separator. Do not change any other formatting.”

Option B — Apply a yellow fill color
“Apply a yellow fill color to the currently selected cells. Keep all other formatting (borders, font, number formats) unchanged.”

Copilot will automatically apply the chosen formatting while preserving all other existing cell properties.

column2: EXCEL - Filter Data
column3:
This use case explains how Copilot in Excel can quickly filter a table based on specific criteria, helping users focus on a relevant subset of data while keeping the underlying dataset and formatting unchanged.
Although the example filters rows where the Region = “NL”, the same logic can be applied to any column or condition, making it useful for all types of segmented analysis.
column4:
Step 1: Select the table or data range you want to filter in Excel.
Step 2: Use this prompt in Copilot:

“Filter the selected table so that it only shows rows where the Region equals ‘NL’. Keep all other data and formatting unchanged.”

Copilot will automatically apply the filter while preserving the rest of the dataset and layout.

column2: EXCEL - Sort Data and Generate a New Sorted Table
column3:
This use case explains how Copilot in Excel can sort an entire table based on a specific column and automatically generate the sorted output in a new worksheet, leaving the original data untouched.
Although the example focuses on sorting by the Delta column, the same process can be used to sort by any metric (Actuals, Variance, Forecast, KPIs, etc.), supporting quick comparisons and cleaner analysis.
column4:
Step 1: Select the table you want to sort in Excel.
Step 2: Use the following prompt in Copilot:

“Sort the entire table by the Delta column from the smallest value to the largest.
Create a new sorted table in a new sheet called ‘Sorted by Delta’, keeping the headers and without modifying the original table.
Show the sorted table preview.”

Copilot will sort the data, generate a new worksheet, and provide a preview while preserving the original table.

column2: EXCEL - Create a Pivot Table
column3:
This use case shows how to use Copilot in Excel to build a pivot table that summarizes Actuals by Country from a specified sheet, and then surface the top 3 countries with the highest Actuals.
The same approach can be reused for other metrics (e.g., Forecast, Variance, Opex) and other dimensions (e.g., Sector, Region, Cost Center).
column4:
Step 1 — Select data
Open sheet xxx and select the data range (or the entire table) you want to summarize.

Step 2 — Ask Copilot to build the pivot
Use this prompt in Copilot:

“Create a pivot table summarizing ‘Actuals’ per ‘Country’ from the selected data in the sheet xxx. Place the pivot in a new worksheet named Pivot – Actuals by Country.”

Step 3 — Ask Copilot to list the Top 3
Then use:

“From that pivot, identify the top 3 Countries with the highest Actuals and show them in a small summary table (Country, Total Actuals) above the pivot.”

What Copilot will do

  • Insert a pivot table with Country in Rows and Actuals summed in Values.
  • Create a new sheet (named as requested).
  • Generate a Top‑3 summary table for quick reference.

column2: EXCEL - Actual vs MOR guardrail analysis with TAP+ buffer & suggested adjustments
column3:
Finance and MOR owners often need to ensure MOR stays within the TAP+ ceiling while maintaining a safety buffer. This use case performs a structured analysis (e.g., on TAB1 of an Excel workbook) over selected rows to:

Compare Actual vs MOR vs TAP+
Apply a configurable buffer to the TAP+ value
Identify where MOR breaches the allowed range
Calculate the required MOR adjustment to comply
Return an actionable table with a column indicating the suggested adjustment and a clear explanation of the calculation logic

This standardizes the process so decisions are quick, traceable, and consistent across MOR cycles.
column4:
Step 1 — Prepare the data

Ensure TAB1 (or the active sheet) contains at least these columns:

Actual — realized value

  • MOR — current plan/forecast to be evaluated
  • TAP+ — upper guardrail/threshold
  • (Optional) Entity / Cost Center / Month to identify rows

Step 3 — Select the rows to evaluate

Step 4  —  run this prompt

“Perform an analysis in the TAB1 tab to verify if the MOR column needs to be adjusted to stay within the TAP+ value. Use a buffer of [X% or fixed amount] and make adjustments accordingly. Use only the selected cells. Return a table with: Actual, MOR, TAP+, Buffer, Max Allowed MOR, Over/Under, Suggested Adjustment, and New MOR. Also include a short explanation of the calculation method used.”

(You can paste your original prompt; the above adds explicit output columns and buffer parameterization.)

Output: table with: Actual, MOR, TAP+, Buffer, Max Allowed MOR, Over/Under, Suggested Adjustment, and New MOR. Also include a short explanation of the calculation method used.

column2: EXCEL - PR ANALYSIS: Top 25 PR Over‑ & Under‑Spend Analysis + Vendor Update Email Trigger
column3:
This use case automatically identifies the top 25 purchase requests (PRs) with the highest overspend and the top 25 with the highest underspend.
It outputs a structured list capturing sector name, PR number, PR/PO value, and vendor name.
The system also generates a standard email text that the PR requester can send to ask the vendor to update the PR (close, lower, or increase).
column4:
Step 1 — Select data

Select data to analyse (table with Pr information)

Step 2 — Apply the prompt

Prompt: "Create a list of all PR with the top 25 over and under spendings. in the list specify sector name, PR number, PR/PO value, Vendor name. Then provide me the ASML email of the vendor with the text of the email to send to request to update PR (close, lower or increase)"
Output: 

1) A table including:

  • Sector name
  • PR number
  • PR/PO value
  • Vendor name

2) An email text template for the vendor

column2: EXCEL - PR ANALYSIS: Filter PRs Older Than 3 Months With High Variance
column3:
This use case filters Purchase Requests (PRs) using two criteria:

PR To‑date older than three months from the fixed reference date 01/02/2026
(threshold: PR To‑date < 01/11/2025)

Absolute Variance on PO value > 100,000

The prompt returns only the rows that meet both conditions, extracts the required columns in order, and creates a new sheet called PR Review with an official Excel Table named Selected_PRs, sorted by Variance descending. The source data remains unchanged.

Note: The prompt can be used with any other filter (e.g., vendor, sector, requester). 
The prompt is useful because it automates filtering, ensures consistency, reduces manual effort, and produces a clean, ready‑to‑use table.
column4:
Step 1 — Select data

Select the data containing PR information (e.g Number, PR To‑date, PO value, variance, vendor name, and requester information).

Step 2 — Set the Fixed Reference Date and Variance 

In this case the starting data is 01/02/2026 (dd/mm/yyyy) as the unchanging reference starting point.

The variance used as a threshold is 100k

Step 3 — Use the prompt

Filter PRs where:
 a. The 'PR To-date' is older than three months, counting months from 01/02/2026 (dd/mm/yyyy). Treat 01/02/2026 as the fixed reference start date.
"Older than three months" means PR To-date < (01/02/2026 - 3 calendar months). b. 'Variance on PO value' > 100,000 (absolute currency value; assume same currency as the dataset).

Return the matching rows as a new, clean table with only these columns, in this order:

PO (this column must contain the PR Number as the user requested)
Date (this is the 'PR To-date' value)
Variance (this is the 'Variance on PO value')
Vendor name
PR Requestor SAP
Rename the output table to 'Selected_PRs' and format it as an official Excel Table.

Sort the output by Variance descending.

Do not modify the source data. Create the result on a new sheet called 'PR Review'.

Output

  • PO → PR Numbe
  • Date → PR To‑date
  • Variance → Variance on PO value
  • Vendor name
  • PR Requestor SAP

column2: EXCEL - Forecasts
column3:
This use case produces a concise executive summary of the MOR forecast using one year of historical quarterly data from the selected Excel sheet (xxx). It identifies key trends, generates forecasts for the next four quarters, outlines assumptions, assesses risks and opportunities, and provides recommendations to support a 10% sales‑growth target.

 This accelerates data analysis and ensures consistent, high‑quality executive reporting.

Note: The same prompt can be applied to any other dataset following the same structure.
column4:
Step 1 — Select data

Import or reference the past‑year quarterly data from the selected Excel sheet (xxx).

Step 2 — Use prompt
Create an executive summary of the MOR forecast based on the historical quarterly data from the last year. Use the selected data in the excel sheet xxx. Include:
1) Key trends observed in historical data
2) Forecasted values for the next 4 quarters
3) Main assumptions behind the forecast
4) Risks and opportunities impacting accuracy
5) Actionable recommendations to achieve a 10% sales growth target.
Present the summary in a concise, professional tone suitable for senior management. Add also a chart displaying a dual-axis line chart comparing actual vs forecasted values.

Output: 

Summary of: 
  • Key trends observed 
  • Forecasted values for the next 4 quarters
  • Main assumptions behind the forecast
  • Risks and opportunities impacting accuracy
  • Actionable recommendations

column2: EXCEL - Pie chart / Bar chart
column3:
This prompt allows to generate a pie chart starting from simple data. In this example the data represents actuals by country, but the same prompt can be applied to any other variable. 

While the prompt produces a pie chart by default, you can also specify different chart types (e.g., bar chart) depending on your needs.
column4:
Step 1 - Select data

Step 2: Apply the prompt

Visualize the ‘total actuals‘ per ‘Country‘ in a pie chart using the data from the selected pivot table in the excel sheet xxx

Output: Pie chart/ bar chart

column2: EXCEL - Stacked chart
column3: The prompt takes the currently selected Excel range and generates a Stacked Column chart by using the first column as the series names and the first row as the Q1–Q4 categories, switching rows and columns if needed. It applies all required formatting and layout rules to ensure the chart follows a precise, standardized structure. The same prompt can be reused with any other dataset that follows the same layout.
column4:
Step 1 - select data

Step2: Apply the prompt

"Use the currently selected range only. Treat the first column as series names (Metric) and the first row as category names (Q1–Q4). If needed, switch rows/columns so that categories are Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 along the x-axis and the series are Committed, MOR, and Remaining.

Use python to create a Stacked Column chart (not 100% stacked) with these exact settings:

DATA & ORDER

Categories (X axis): Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 only. Series (order): Committed, MOR, Remaining. Vertical stack from bottom to top: Committed (bottom), MOR (middle), Remaining (top). TITLE & LEGEND

Values: Y axis

Title: CM – Committed spend 2025 Legend at bottom, in this order and captions: Committed (blue), Remaining (gray), MOR (red) COLORS

Show values only (no series/category) Position: inside center for Committed and Remaining; inside end or center for MOR LAYOUT

The visible stack is Committed (blue) → MOR (red) → Remaining (gray) Legend reads Committed (blue), Remaining (gray), MOR (red) Tip: If Copilot draws clustered columns, immediately say: “Change chart type to Stacked Column (not 100%). Then reorder the stack: bottom Committed, middle MOR, top Remaining.”
column6:
This use case explains how Copilot in Excel can quickly apply specific formatting to selected cells, helping users standardize their spreadsheets and improve data readability.
The example shows how to format numbers as Euro currency or apply a yellow fill color, but the same method can be used for any formatting action, such as borders, fonts, number formats, alignment, and cell styles.

column2: WORD - Review and Edit with Copilot in Word.
column3: This prompt reviews and improves written text to make it clearer, more coherent, and more engaging.
column4:
Step 1: Open Microsoft Word

The user opens a Word document and selects the text they want to improve.

Step 2: Provide Input to Copilot

The user highlights the text and opens Copilot in Word.

Step 3: Use the Prompt

The user enters the prompt into Copilot: “Please review and improve the following text. Focus on enhancing the content for clarity, coherence, and engagement while ensuring proper grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Use simple business English to make the text accessible and professional. If any part of the text is unclear, suggest better phrasing or structure. Maintain the original intent, but refine it to make it polished, concise, and suitable for a business audience.”

Step 4: Copilot Generates the Output

Copilot rewrites the text, producing a clearer and more structured version, improved grammar and punctuation, a consistent professional tone, suggestions for unclear or weak parts, and a polished result ready for use in documents, emails, or presentations

Step 5: User Reviews and Applies Changes

The user reviews the improved text and decides whether to accept the full rewrite, apply only specific parts, or ask Copilot to refine it further

column2: WORD - Summary with Copilot in Word
column3: With this prompt users can quickly generate a concise, one‑paragraph summary of any text or file using Copilot.
column4:
Step 1: Open Microsoft Word/Copilot App

The user opens Microsoft Word or Microsoft Copilot (depending on where the content is stored) and selects or uploads the text or file they want summarized.

Step 2: Provide the Input

The input can be a block of text, a pasted section of a document, or a file (e.g., Word, PDF, or another supported format)

Step 3: Use the Prompt in Copilot

The user opens Copilot and enters the prompt: “What is a concise, one-paragraph summary of the key concepts, context, and implications of the following text or file?”

Step 4: Copilot Generates the Output

Copilot produces a single, clear paragraph explaining the context and capturing the main ideas. The output is written in simple, professional language for easy understanding.

Step 5: User Reviews and Applies the Summary

The user can then insert the summary into a document, use it to brief colleagues, copy it into an email, message, or meeting notes, or request a shorter, longer, or more detailed version

column2: POWER POINT - Adding speaker notes to slides
column3: This prompt helps users create straightforward speaker notes for their PowerPoint slides.
column4:
Step 1: Open PowerPoint

The user opens the presentation and selects the slide that needs speaker notes.

Step 2: Provide the Input

The slide content (text or visuals) is the input.
Optional: the user can add extra context if needed.

Step 3: Use the Prompt in Copilot

The user opens Copilot in PowerPoint and enters: “Generate clear speaker notes for this slide. Summarize the main message and explain what the presenter should say in easy-to-follow points.”

Step 4: Copilot Generates the Output

Copilot produces short, clear speaker notes and key points the presenter should cover.

Step 5: User Reviews and Inserts Notes

The user reviews the notes and inserts them directly, edits them as needed, or requests a different style (shorter, more formal, etc.)

column2: POWER POINT - Creating diagrams for slides
column3: This prompt helps users generate professional diagrams or tables that visually support the information on their PowerPoint slides.
column4:
Step 1: Open PowerPoint

The user opens Microsoft PowerPoint and selects the slide where a table or diagram is needed.

Step 2: Provide the Input

The input may include slide text, bullet points, data, or a brief explanation of what needs to be visualized.

Step 3: Use the Prompt in Copilot

The user opens Copilot and enters: “Create a diagram or table that expands on the information in this slide. Use a professional style that fits naturally into the presentation.”

Step 4: Copilot Generates the Output

Copilot produces a recommended diagram (e.g., flowchart, process model, timeline), structured table summarizing the key information, a layout that fits the slide’s design and stays visually consistent, or optional improvements or alternative formats.

Step 5: User Reviews and Inserts the Visual

The user can insert the diagram/table directly, adjust colors or layout, ask Copilot for variations (simpler, more detailed, different layout), or use the result to enhance clarity and impact of the slide.

column2: OUTLOOK AND TEAMS - Extracting and Classifying (MOR/Forecast) Relevant Messages from Outlook & Teams
column3:
This use case automates the retrieval, filtering, and classification of all Outlook and Teams messages (both received and sent) that relate to the MOR/forecasting process.
Using a checklist of defined MOR‑related terms, the system scans the user’s email (including Sent Items) and Teams chats/channels from October 15 until today.
For each message that contains a relevant term or its synonym, the system extracts required metadata and produces a structured table with 5 columns:

Short description of the item
Detailed description
Sender
Direct link to the Outlook email or Teams message
Source (Email or Teams)

If a message contains a relevant term but the context is unclear, it is still included and labeled “context unclear.” 

NOTE: The use case is focused on MOR forecasting process but it is also suitable to other topics
column4:
Step 1 —Provide the MOR/forecasting terminology checklist file (if available)


Step 2 — Prompt

Use this prompt on Copilot chat:

 "Use the attached file as a checklist of terms relevant to the MOR/forecasting process. 

Create a table with 5 columns: 1. Short description of the relevant item. 2. More detailed description. 3. Who sent the message. 4. Direct link to the email or Teams message. 5. Indicate whether the source is Email or Teams.

Include:

 - All messages (received and sent) that contain any of the terms or synonyms listed in the checklist. 

- Consider both Outlook (including the Sent Items folder) and Teams. - 

Time range: from October 15 until today. 


- If the content is not clearly relevant but contains a term from the checklist, include it with the note “context unclear”."

OUTPUT:

A table including:

  • Short description (auto‑generated summary)
  • Detailed description (extended summary or parsed content)
  • Sender
  • Direct link (Outlook deeplink or Teams permalink)
  • Source indicator (Email / Teams)
Short DescriptionDetailed DescriptionWho SentLinkSource


If no messages match, return an empty table with headers.

column2: POWER POINT - Creating PowerPoint deck based on memo
column3: This prompt shows how Copilot in PowerPoint can transform an internal advisory memo into a fully structured, professionally formatted presentation.
column4:
Step 1: Input Document

Open the memo in a Word document and save it to your OneDrive.

Step 2: User Prompt in PowerPoint
Open PowerPoint in Copilot Chat and use this prompt: “Create a new presentation using the professional layout, style, and color palette. Use data from [file]. Follow these slide‑by‑slide instructions: Slide 1: Title of memo; From: [Job title] Date: [Early 2026]; Subject: [Subject of memo]; Period: Current and future years Slide 2: Executive Summary Slide 3: [Content from memo section 3] Slide 4: [Content from memo section 4] Slide 5: [Content from memo section 5] Slide 6: Conclusion. Deliverable: A complete, well‑formatted PPT.”

Step 3: Copilot Processing

Copilot automatically reads the content of the memo, maps memo sections to the slide structure provided, extracts relevant data, tables, and narrative, applies: corporate‑style templates, consistent color palette, and clarity‑optimized text summarization, and creates a complete multi-slide deck aligned with the memo’s structure and tone.

Step 4: Output Delivered
A polished, presentation‑ready PowerPoint file and is ready for review, internal distribution, or external communication.

Step 5: Troubleshooting

​If there are random images and you want only text to apply ASML template later, use this prompt: "Use only text for the PowerPoint."

  • Output: blank PPT with only text.

column2: OUTLOOK AND TEAMS - Weekly to‑do list generation from notes and emails using Copilot
column3: This prompt shows how Copilot can analyze a user’s recent emails and meeting/notes content to automatically generate a focused, actionable weekly to‑do list.
column4:
Step 1: User Prompt in Outlook or Copilot App

Open the app Outlook, Copilot Chat, or OneNote and use this prompt: “Create a to‑do list for the week based on my recent notes and emails.”

Step 2: Copilot Processing

Copilot automatically scans the user’s recent emails for: action requests, approvals needed, upcoming deadlines, and open threads requiring responses. Copilot reviews recent notes for: meeting follow‑ups, decisions requiring completion, and tasks assigned to the user. Then Copilot groups and organizes all tasks into a cohesive weekly list.

Step 4: Output Delivered
Output: A structured weekly to‑do list. The output can be inserted into: an email, a task list (Microsoft To Do), a meeting prep document, or a summary reply to the user.

column2: WORD - Turn rough notes into detailed notes with Copilot
column3: This prompt shows how Copilot can take unstructured notes and transform them into clear, concise, professionally worded thematic summaries.
column4:
Step 1: User Prompt in Copilot
Open Copilot Chat, Word, or OneNote and use this prompt: “Review the notes above and organize them into clear, logical themes. Rephrase fragmented or informal points into usable professional wording without adding new information. Explicitly flag gaps, ambiguities, assumptions, and inconsistencies. Identify risks, open questions, or items that require follow‑up. Keep the output concise and suitable for internal alignment or documentation.”

Step 2: Copilot Processing

Copilot automatically reads the notes and identifies natural groupings such as topics, workstreams, decisions, or concerns, rewrites informal or partial statements into clear, coherent professional language, preserves the original meaning without inventing new content, and detects and explicitly flags: missing information or incomplete logic, conflicts or unclear statements, overlooked assumptions, or dependencies or contradictions. Copilot structures the output into a concise, usable summary suitable for internal distribution.

Step 4: Output Delivered
The output is a clean, organized thematic summary. The output can be inserted into: a decision log, a project brief, a meeting readout, or a documentation package for team alignment.

column2: WORD - Adding APA references to paragraph
column3: This prompt demonstrates how Copilot can make a better paragraph by locating relevant source material, inserting appropriate in‑text citations, and generating a full reference list in APA format.
column4:
Step 1: User Prompt in Word or Copilot
Open Word with Copilot or Copilot Chat. Use this prompt: “Add references to this paragraph and also insert the list of references in APA format.”

Step 2: Copilot Processing

Copilot automatically inserts in‑text citations following APA rules, creates a reference list section at the end of the document, and formats each reference according to APA style requirements. Copilot ensures consistency and avoids adding unsupported or fabricated information and highlights any gaps where no supporting source could be found.

Step 3: Output Delivered
The output is a citation‑enhanced, APA‑compliant paragraph. Optional annotations (if requested) for: missing evidence, claims requiring further sourcing, or areas needing clarification or stronger support. The result is ready for internal reports, research notes, and documentation.

column2: EXCEL - Bridge chart
column3: the prompt is able to generate bridge charts, which is useful to explain sequential changes in data in a simple and visual way.
column4:
Step 1 - Crerate your Excel table

Make sure your dataset is clean and correctly structured before creating the chart.
Check that:

  • the data contains no duplicates, no NaN values, and no blank cells
  • the range is formatted as an Excel Table
  • the table includes exactly these three columns:

label – the category name shown on the chart
value – the numeric amount to be plotted
type – either total or step, indicating the role of each bar in the waterfall

TIP: use the prompt in the library to transform your data

Step 2 - Select the data

Step 3 - Apply the prompt

Create a Waterfall chart from the CURRENT TABLE with columns [label, value, type].
Map type="total" → Start (first row) and End (last row); map type="step" → intermediate steps.
Keep the current row order. Insert the chart directly below the table on this sheet.

column2: TEAMS - Summarize meetings
column3: This prompt shows how to use Copilot to transform Teams meeting transcripts into high-quality, structured minutes.
column4:
Step 1: Start Transcripts in Microsoft Teams
Start by enabling transcription in Microsoft Teams to capture the meeting notes in real time. 

Step 2: Download Transcript after Meeting
Once the meeting ends, download the transcript file from Teams in .txt or .docx format for further processing. 

Step 3: Upload to Copilot & generate initial MoM
Upload the transcript to M365 Copilot Chat and use this prompt to generate a structured summary with key decisions, action items, and follow-ups.
Prompt: 'Here is a transcript of a meeting. Turn this into a professional but concise meeting summary, extract action points, and assign follow-ups as if I were a manager delegating to a team. Keep it concise but emphasize the topics that had longer discussions.' 

Step 4: Fine-tune the Output
Review the summary and provide feedback to Copilot to refine the content.

column2: TEAMS - Prepare meeting objective, agenda and engagement approach
column3: These prompts help with the preparation of meetings.
column4:
Step 1: Define the Meeting Objective
Establish a clear understanding of why the meeting is being held and what success looks like. Include context, meeting type and desired outcomes.
Prompt: 'Create a clear and concise objective for upcoming meeting with [team/client/stakeholder] about [insert draft proposal/goal]. The goal is to ensure everyone understands the purpose and expected outcomes.' 

Step 2: Structure the Agenda of the Meeting
Create a time-efficient plan that ensures all key topics are covered..
Prompt: 'Create a structured agenda for a [duration]-minute meeting on [topic]. Include time slots, discussion points, presenters, and a buffer for Q&A or wrap-up.' 

Step 3: Engagement Plan
Upload the meeting goals and agenda to Copilot Chat, then use a tailored prompt to generate the most effective meeting format and engagement tools.
Prompt: 'Here is the background/goal of a meeting. Recommend an engagement approach for a meeting focused on [goal and agenda]. Include the best format, tools for collaboration, and techniques to keep participants actively involved.'
column6: Copilot may generate repetitive agendas that lack strategic depth/creativity. Customize prompts with specific goals, team dynamics, recent developments etc.

column2: OUTLOOK - Summarize emails
column3: This prompts shows how Copilot can quickly process 100+ emails.
column4: Prompt: 'Process all unread emails since [date] and categorize them by project or team. Per category, include latest status and action points with owners and deadlines.'

column2: OUTLOOK - Drafting emails with Copilot
column3: These prompts help users efficiently draft clear, professional emails and improve tone and clarity using Copilot in Outlook.
column4:
Step 1: Create a first draft of the email

Use Copilot to generate a complete first version of an email based on context, purpose, audience, and desired tone. This is especially useful when starting from a blank page or when time is limited.

Prompt: 'Draft a professional email to [stakeholder/team] explaining [financial topic, decision, or variance]. The tone should be clear, concise, and appropriate for a finance audience.'

Step 2: Review and refine using Coaching by Copilot

After drafting the email (either manually or with Copilot), use Coaching by Copilot to receive feedback on tone, clarity, structure, and professionalism. This helps ensure the message is well‑balanced and fit for the audience.

Prompt: 'Coach this email. Suggest improvements to clarity, tone, and structure while keeping the message professional and concise.'

column2: TEAMS - A daily overview of actions and priorities
column3: This use case helps users quickly understand their key actions, priorities, and open topics by summarizing emails, meetings, and chats across Outlook and Teams.
column4:
Step 1: Get an overview of outstanding actions
Use Copilot to review emails and Teams messages and identify open actions, follow‑ups, and priorities.

Prompt: 'Looking at my emails and Teams chats, give me an overview of my outstanding actions from yesterday.'

Step 2: Focus on what needs attention today
Refine the overview to highlight urgent or important items.

Prompt: 'Highlight the most important or time‑sensitive actions I should focus on today.'

column2: TEAMS - Schedule meetings efficiently with Copilot
column3: This use case helps users schedule meetings more efficiently by using Copilot to identify suitable time slots, reduce back‑and‑forth communication, and create meeting invitations in Outlook and Teams.
column4:
Step 1: Identify suitable time slots

Use Copilot to review calendar availability and suggest suitable time slots for a meeting.

Prompt: 'Show me available time slots this week for a [time]-minute meeting with [person/team].'

Step 2: Select the most appropriate option

Review the suggested options and choose the time that best fits the participants’ availability and urgency of the topic.

Step 3: Create and schedule the meeting

Use Copilot to create the meeting invitation, including the meeting title and basic context. The meeting can be scheduled as a Teams meeting directly from Outlook.

Prompt: 'Schedule a Teams meeting with [person/team] at the selected time and include the topic [topic].'

column2: EXCEL - Comment tables
column3:
This use case enables users to generate comments and insights directly on their tables, supporting data analysis and significantly improving efficiency.

The use case is currently based on MOR data, comparing Actuals with TAP+ values, but it can be easily adapted to other datasets and analytical scenarios.
column4:
Step 1 - Select the table 

Step 2 - Apply the following prompt

make comments and assumptions regarding the selected data focusing on 'Total primary costs' of YTD, Q1 and FY26 (row 24). you know that Actual is what actually was spent, TAP+ is the budget that was planned, Delta is Actuals - TAP+. Then look at the different expenses and try to make assumptions on given Total primary costs comments. For instance: YTD Total primary costs underspend/overspend of .... vs YTD TAP+; main drivers are: Brand & Digital: ...... Corp Comms: .... THIS IS JUST AN EXAMPLE. KEEP THE STRUCTURE FOR WRITING THE COMMENT BUT CHANGE THE CONTENT. MAKE IT MORE DEEPER ANALYZED

column2: WORD - Explanation of regulation/articles with Copilot
column3: This prompt helps users quickly understand complex regulations, rulings, or legal articles by transforming them into simple, practical summaries.
column4:
Step 1: Input the Regulation or Legal Text (Microsoft Word or Copilot Chat)
Open Word or Copilot (Chat). Paste the full text of a regulation, ruling, or article you want summarized.
Example:
Paste Article 5 of the GDPR.

Step 2: Prompt Copilot
Example Prompt:
**“Explain the core concepts of the following regulation in simple terms. Include:
plain English summary
risks
meeting questions
business impact.”**
(Then paste the regulation.)

Step 3: Copilot Generates a Clear Summary
Copilot produces a simplified explanation of the regulation, key risks or compliance issues, questions to ask in meetings, and practical business impact assessment. This enables teams to digest legal content without relying solely on legal experts.
Step 4: User Reviews and Iterates
The user refines the response by asking follow‑up prompts such as: “Make it shorter.”, “Create a slide with the key messages.”, and “Add an example relevant to regulation." This gives tailored content suitable for presentations, documentation, or decision‑making.

column2: POWER POINT - PPT Decks with Excel Data
column3: Creates a fully formatted  PowerPoint by reusing an existing template, importing Excel data, standardizing tables and charts, updating month references, and producing a delivery checklist and change log.
column4:
Step 1 - Prepare the sources

Ensure that both the PowerPoint template (for design, layout, and styling) and the Excel files (as data sources) are prepared and available before starting. This means that the Excel file has to contain exactly the same tables to insert in the new power point. 

Step 2 - Create your deck

In Copilot chat upload your Power point and excel file then apply the following prompt:

Create a new presentation named “CM MT December Update.pptx”.


Apply the exact master, layouts, fonts, color palette, alignment rules, and spacing from the file “Comms Finance Update Dec ’25.pptx”. Use the same slide master as presentation default and apply it to all slides.


Use the Excel workbook “book1.xlsx” as the only data source. Sheets to use:

- “YTD”

- “OPEX Summary table”  (note: not “Opex”; use the exact sheet name)

- “YTD Regions table”

- “Travel & Training table”


If a sheet named “OPEX Summary graph” exists, do NOT summarize it; ignore for now unless asked later.


Global data hygiene and transformation rules (APPLY TO ALL TABLES):

- Do NOT summarize, aggregate, pivot, or hide data.

- Do NOT delete any row/column unless it contains ONLY NaN/blanks/dashes/empty cells. If removed, log it for the change log.

- Replace any generic column headers like “Column1, Column2, …” with BLANK headers (empty string), not “—” or “N/A”.

- Update all textual month references from “Oct/Nov” to “Nov/Dec” everywhere in imported content.

- Numeric normalization: render all numeric cells with a maximum of 2 decimals; integers must have no trailing “.00”.

- Keep ALL rows and ALL columns that contain any non-empty value.


Slide plan (create exactly these slides):

1) Slide 1 — Title Only

   Title: “Financial Update December 2025”

2) Slide 2 — Title + Content

   Title: “Financial Update December 2025”

   Subtitle: “Communication”

   Content: Insert the FULL table from sheet “YTD”, after applying the global rules.

3) Slide 3 — Title + Content

   Title: “Q4 Communication OPEX”

   Content: Insert the FULL table from sheet “OPEX Summary table”, after applying the global rules.

4) Slide 4 — Title + Content

   Title: “OPEX YTD per region”

   Content: Insert the FULL table from sheet “YTD Regions table”.

   Only remove rows/columns that are entirely NaN/blank/dash; keep all other data.

5) Slide 5 — Title + Content

   Title: “Travel spend actuals”

   Content: Insert the FULL table from sheet “Travel & Training table”, after applying the global rules.


Technical import requirements:

- Import each range as a native PowerPoint table (not an image). Preserve all rows and columns.

- Preserve text as text; do not convert to images.

- Do not add any commentary, callouts, insights, or summaries in the slides at this stage.

Deliverable for this step:

- A presentation named exactly “CM MT December Update.pptx”, with all 5 slides created and all tables inserted strictly 1:1 (no summarization).


Step 3 - Improve table format and layout

In Copilot chat upload your presentation (output of STEP2) AND apply the following prompt:

Work on the currently open file “CM MT December Update.pptx”.

Formatting rules to enforce on ALL imported tables (all slides):

- Do NOT delete any row/column unless it contains ONLY NaN/blanks/dashes/empty cells.

- Replace generic headers like “Column1, Column2, ...” with BLANK headers (empty string).

- Numeric cells: maximum 2 decimals; show integers without “.00”.

- Wrapping: ENABLE text wrapping for headers/labels; DISABLE text wrapping for numeric cells (numbers must fit on one horizontal line).

- Alignment: left-align text cells; right-align numeric cells.

- Typography: set all table text to 7 pt. Reduce padding, spacing, and borders to the minimum consistent with the master.

- Width logic: first Apply AutoFit-to-contents; then distribute column widths evenly; finally scale/resize the table so the FULL table fits entirely inside the slide margins (no overflow or cropped content).

- Apply the same fonts, colors, alignment, and spacing as the master/theme from “Comms Finance Update Dec ’25.pptx”.

Per slide actions:

- Slide 2 (“Financial Update December 2025” / “Communication”): enforce all the above.

- Slide 3 (“Q4 Communication OPEX”): enforce all the above.

- Slide 4 (“OPEX YTD per region”): remove ONLY rows/columns that are entirely NaN/blank/dash, then enforce all the above and make sure the entire table fits on one slide.

- Slide 5 (“Travel spend actuals”): enforce all the above.

Quality controls (must execute and then add a summary slide at the end):

1) Verify no numeric cell wraps to a second line; if it does, reduce padding/spacing/border thickness first, then reduce text size uniformly down to 7 pt (do not go below 7 pt), then expand the table area within the margins if needed.

2) Verify there is NO truncated content and NO hidden columns or rows.

3) Verify that month references were updated from “Oct/Nov” to “Nov/Dec” across all tables and titles we control.

4) Verify that any generic “Column#” headers are blank.

Create three additional slides at the end:

A) “Checklist of updates” — bullet list per slide (2–5) describing exactly which rules were applied and confirming fit-to-margins success.

B) “Change log” — for each slide, list:

   - which Excel sheet was used,

   - count of rows/columns removed because they were entirely empty/dash/NaN,

   - count of generic headers replaced with blank,

   - number of textual “Oct/Nov → Nov/Dec” replacements performed.

C) “Flags & suggestions” — list any missing or ambiguous data (e.g., placeholder headers, unclear column names, extremely wide matrices), and propose either placeholders or alternative visuals (e.g., appendix slide, heatmap, or Top-N deltas) WITHOUT changing or summarizing the main tables.

Important:

- Do NOT add insights or commentary beyond the requested checklist/log/flags.

- Keep the deck using the master of “Comms Finance Update Dec ’25.pptx”.


 Step 4: Improve the design of your powerpoint deck

Open the power point deck (output STEP3) on Power Point app 

Click on "design suggestions"

column2: EXCEL - Adding a dynamic summary row (filter‑aware) to an Excel table
column3:
This prompt helps you create a dynamic summary row that automatically updates when slicers or filters are applied to an Excel table.
The summary is placed outside the table so the table structure remains intact.
column4:
1. ​Open Excel Copilot (Agent Mode opens automatically).
  2. Put in the following prompt: "Using the table [table_name]: Create a single summary row outside the table labeled "[Label]". For each numeric column, calculate the SUM of the visible rows only (filter-aware). Do NOT add this row inside the table. Do NOT modify the existing table structure. Place the summary row directly below the table on the same sheet." ​

column2: OUTLOOK - Priority & Action List Creation with Copilo
column3: This use case helps employees quickly regain control after periods of high workload, absence, or context switching by using Copilot to review recent Microsoft 365 activity.
column4:
Step 1 – Review recent work activity

  • App: Copilot Chat (Microsoft 365)
  • Input: A Copilot prompt specifying a timeframe (e.g. past 1–4 weeks)
  • Example Prompt:

    " Review my Microsoft 365 activity for the past [X weeks] across:

    (1) Outlook emails, (2) Teams chats + channel posts, (3) Meetings from my calendar (including meeting Recap, transcripts when available, meeting chat, and any meeting notes), (4) files I viewed/edited, and (5) my tasks (To Do / Planner) if available.

    Goal: Create a project/workstream list and a prioritized action plan so I can quickly catch up and start with the most important work.

    Instructions:

    1.  Cluster items into projects/workstreams (merge duplicates across email/chat/meetings/files).

    2.  For each project/workstream, provide:

      What it is / objective (1–2 lines)

      Current status (what’s moving / blocked)

      Key stakeholders (people or teams involved)

      Open actions (clear, verb-first tasks) and who owns them (me vs others)

      Deadlines or time signals (dates mentioned, urgency language, escalations)

    3.  Create a Top Priorities (1–5) section, ranked by urgency + impact, and a Start Today list with the first next step for each.

    4.  Add a section: ‘Meeting-derived actions & decisions’ (capture decisions, commitments, and follow-ups that came from meetings). "

Step 2 – Structure work into projects and priorities

  • App: Copilot Chat
  • What Copilot Does:
    • Clusters information into projects/workstreams
    • Removes duplicates across emails, meetings, chats, and files
    • Identifies objectives, current status, key stakeholders, deadlines, and open actions

Step 3 – Generate an actionable priority plan

  • Output:
    • A Top Priorities (1–5) list ranked by urgency and impact
    • A “Start Today” task list with clear next steps
    • A Meeting‑derived actions & decisions section capturing commitments and follow‑ups

Step 4 – Use output for daily planning and follow‑up

  • Outcome:
    • User uses the Copilot output as a daily or weekly action plan
    • Reduces time spent manually reviewing emails and meeting notes
    • Improves follow‑up and accountability

column2: EXCEL - XLOOKUP
column3: The use case is useful to transfer data from a source to new destination using XLOOKUP. A combined key is used, and formulas are automatically filled down for all rows. In the video the use case is used to transfer data from Costplan tab to a new tab. However the use case can be used to replace XLOOKUP in any case.
column4:
STEP 0: data source

Before anything, make sure you have:

  • A source sheet/table (e.g., Costplan)
  • Clear headers (column names are consistent)
  • A column (or combination of columns) that can work as a unique key
  • No messy merged headers inside the data area (data should start on a clean header row)

STEP1: create or reuse the destination sheet

  • If the sheet already exists, reuse it
  • If not, create a new sheet

STEP2: Run Copilot in Excel and paste the following prompt, replacing info in brackets with the name of your new sheet:

In **[DESTINATION_SHEET]**, build (or update) a table in **[DEST_RANGE]** with headers: 

**[HEADER_1], [HEADER_2], … [HEADER_N]**.

 

### 1) Create the row list (unique keys)

From **[SOURCE_SHEET]**, take rows where **[FILTER_CONDITION]** is true. 

Create **one row per unique [KEY_FIELD]** (or per unique combined key). 

Fill base columns (e.g., ID, Name, Description) from the source. 

Sort by **[SORT_FIELD]**.

 

### 2) Fill columns using XLOOKUP (single or combined key)

Define the lookup key:

 

- **Key in destination:** **[DEST_KEY]** (e.g., `[@ID]&[@Region]`) 

- **Key in source:** **[SRC_KEY]** (e.g., `Source!$A$2:$A$999 & Source!$B$2:$B$999`)

 

For each output column, use:

```excel

=XLOOKUP([DEST_KEY], [SRC_KEY], [RETURN_RANGE], [DEFAULT_IF_NOT_FOUND], 0)

```

Fill the formula down for all rows.

 

```

 

### 4) Final checks

Ensure formulas are filled down, formats are correct (number/currency), and there are no errors like **#N/A** unless expected.
